summary: Joel Spolsky discusses the financial and practical challenges of building out office space, particularly the tax disadvantage of leasehold improvements which can't be meaningfully deducted. He explains how his company outgrew their current office and faces the prospect of spending millions on construction for a new 15,000 square foot space. He reveals why companies build cubicle farms instead of real walls: office furniture depreciates faster and can be leased, making it tax-deductible. His proposed solution is manufactured, modular wall systems from Haworth that qualify as furniture rather than leasehold improvements while still providing private offices.
key_points: Leasehold improvements are barely tax-deductible (depreciated over 39 years), while office furniture depreciates over 7 years and can be leased with fully deductible payments
The tax code creates a perverse incentive where companies choose cubicle farms over private offices not because they're cheaper to build, but because of how they're classified for depreciation
Manufactured modular wall systems offer a workaround: they provide real private offices but qualify as moveable furniture rather than permanent construction
Building out office space is also environmentally wasteful since the next tenant will likely demolish everything and start over
money_quotes: This is why companies build cubicle farms instead of walls, even though the dollar cost is comparable.
You have $0 in the bank, but the IRS thinks you made a $20 profit.
To build that much office space could cost a couple of million dollars. With the lack of deductability, your bank account goes down by three million dollars.
All in all, it's a big waste of money and environmentally unsound.
Please do not take tax advice from me. I am morbidly unqualified to give tax advice.
key_insight: The US tax code's distinction between leasehold improvements and office furniture is the hidden reason companies build cubicle farms instead of private offices, but modular wall systems offer a clever workaround that provides real offices with better tax treatment.
